---
title: "Benchmark (diseasenowcasting vs NobBS, epinowcast and baselinenowcast)"
author: "diseasenowcasting team"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
bibliography: references.bib
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Benchmark (diseasenowcasting vs NobBS, epinowcast and baselinenowcast)}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  message  = FALSE,
  warning  = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 9,
  fig.height = 4.5,
  fig.align='center',
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>"
)
```


> **NOTE:** THIS VIGNETTE IS UNDER DEVELOPMENT. 

## TL; DR

**Does `diseasenowcasting` actually work?** On three real outbreaks â€” dengue,
mpox and COVID-19 â€” we asked each method to estimate the most recent, still-
incomplete case counts, and then checked those estimates against the totals that
*eventually* arrived. `diseasenowcasting` was **consistently as accurate as, and
usually more accurate than**, three established nowcasting packages (NobBS,
epinowcast and baselinenowcast). It was also consistently **faster** 

> Its `HSGP/LogNormal` and `HSGP/GeneralizedGamma` models are the most reliable
hence the ones we recommend. 


## What this benchmark does 

A nowcast estimates **what the recent case counts will turn out to be** once all
the delayed reports have come in. This benckmark:

1. Picks a past date and hides everything reported after it (so the method only
   sees what a real-time analyst would have seen).
2. Asks each method to nowcast the most recent date.
3. Compares that nowcast to the count that **eventually** got reported.
4. Repeats over **50 dates per disease** and summarises.

We do this for three diseases with different reporting behaviours, and against
three widely used tools:

| Disease | Data | Time unit | Compared against |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dengue fever | `denguedat` (Puerto Rico) [@mcgough2020nowcasting] | Weekly | NobBS, epinowcast, baselinenowcast |
| Mpox | `mpoxdat` (USA) [@rohrer2025nowcasting] | Daily | NobBS, epinowcast, baselinenowcast |
| COVID-19 | `covid_colombia` [@colombia] | Daily | NobBS, epinowcast, baselinenowcast |

[**NobBS**](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/NobBS/index.html),
[**epinowcast**](https://github.com/epinowcast/epinowcast) and
[**baselinenowcast**](https://github.com/epinowcast/baselinenowcast) are the 
established nowcasting packages we compare against. 

### How to read the score tables

Each method gives a prediction interval for the eventual count. We score it 
with the **Weighted Interval Score (WIS)**, one of the standard metrics in nowcasting and
forecasting challenges.

> The one thing to remember: **lower WIS is better.** 

We also report **coverage**: how often the truth actually falls inside the stated
prediction intervals. A well-calibrated method's **90% interval should contain the truth
about 90% of the time**. 


### How was it compared

> **Note**  Each method is scored only on the dates where it *and*
`diseasenowcasting` both produced a nowcast (a "common date set"). A package
that occasionally fails to fit therefore only shrinks *its own* comparison, never
the whole benchmark. A note under each table reports who failed and how
often. Where a package offers several options (e.g. epinowcast's reporting-
delay choices), we show the ones that fit most reliably.

We tried **epinowcast** with the Log-normal, Log-logistic and Gamma delay distributions, 
plus a **non-parametric** delay. We utilized the `Pathfinder` option on
**epinowcast** as **sampling** was too slow for a realistic comparison. 

**baselinenowcast and NobBS packages are run as designed.** They
are run _as is_. 


## Results

The pre-computed scores ship with the package, so these tables reproduce exactly.

```{r load-data, echo = FALSE}
library(diseasenowcasting)
library(dplyr)

# Locate a pre-computed result file: installed package -> source tree -> repo.
# This lets the vignette knit before the package is installed (pkgdown, R CMD
# build) and on the website.
find_rds <- function(name) {
  local <- c(system.file("extdata", name, package = "diseasenowcasting"),
             file.path("../inst/extdata", name), file.path("inst", "extdata", name))
  local <- local[nzchar(local) & file.exists(local)]
  if (length(local)) return(local[1])
  base <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/RodrigoZepeda/diseasenowcasting"
  for (ref in c("master", "main")) {
    tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
    ok  <- tryCatch({
      utils::download.file(paste0(base, "/", ref, "/inst/extdata/", name),
                           tmp, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE)
      file.exists(tmp) && file.size(tmp) > 0
    }, error = function(e) FALSE)
    if (isTRUE(ok)) return(tmp)
  }
  NA_character_
}

scores_file <- find_rds("benchmark_scores.rds")
conv_file   <- find_rds("benchmark_convergence.rds")
scores_df   <- if (!is.na(scores_file)) readRDS(scores_file) else NULL
conv_df     <- if (!is.na(conv_file))   readRDS(conv_file)   else NULL

# Defensive: if a run ever included auto_nowcast() (automatic model selection),
# drop it -- it is not part of these head-to-head accuracy tables.
if (!is.null(scores_df)) scores_df <- scores_df[scores_df$model != "own auto_nowcast", , drop = FALSE]
if (!is.null(conv_df))   conv_df   <- conv_df[conv_df$model   != "own auto_nowcast", , drop = FALSE]

# The vignette expects the per-view schema (a `view` column).
have_scores <- !is.null(scores_df) && "view" %in% names(scores_df)
```

```{r table-helpers, echo = FALSE}
# Render one scored view (own / nobbs / epinowcast / baselinenowcast) for a
# disease as a kable, captioned with the number of common evaluation dates.  Each
# value column is formatted to a string and the BEST cell(s) are wrapped in
# <strong> so they render bold (escape = FALSE).  Which cells are "best":
#   * WIS / Overprediction / Underprediction / Dispersion / Time: ALL rows tied
#     for the lowest, compared at the DISPLAYED precision (so two rows showing the
#     same rounded number are both bolded -- not just one);
#   * Bias: all rows tied for closest to 0 (at two decimals);
#   * Cov50 / Cov90: every row that is calibrated-or-conservative, i.e. AT OR
#     ABOVE the nominal level (>= 0.50 / >= 0.90).
view_table <- function(dis, view_name, caption) {
  tab <- scores_df[scores_df$disease == dis & scores_df$view %in% view_name, , drop = FALSE]
  tab <- tab |> dplyr::distinct(model, .keep_all = TRUE) |> dplyr::arrange(wis)
  if (nrow(tab) == 0) return(invisible(NULL))
  n_dates <- tab$common_dates[1]

  tied_low <- function(x, digits)                                   # all tied for the min
    which(round(x, digits) == round(min(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits))
  tied_0   <- function(x)                                           # all tied for closest to 0
    which(round(abs(x), 2) == round(min(abs(x), na.rm = TRUE), 2))
  at_least <- function(x, level) which(x >= level - 1e-9)           # at or above nominal
  col <- function(x, digits, best) {                               # format + bold the best cells
    s <- formatC(round(x, digits) + 0, format = "f", digits = digits)  # +0 drops "-0.00"
    s[best] <- paste0("<strong>", s[best], "</strong>")
    s
  }

  out <- data.frame(
    Model           = tab$model,
    WIS             = col(tab$wis,   1, tied_low(tab$wis,   1)),
    Overprediction  = col(tab$over,  1, tied_low(tab$over,  1)),
    Underprediction = col(tab$under, 1, tied_low(tab$under, 1)),
    Dispersion      = col(tab$disp,  1, tied_low(tab$disp,  1)),
    Bias            = col(tab$bias,  2, tied_0(tab$bias)),
    Cov50           = col(tab$cov50, 2, at_least(tab$cov50, 0.50)),
    Cov90           = col(tab$cov90, 2, at_least(tab$cov90, 0.90)),
    check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  if ("secs" %in% names(tab))
    out[["Time (s)"]] <- col(tab$secs, 2, tied_low(tab$secs, 2))

  knitr::kable(out, row.names = FALSE, escape = FALSE,
               caption = sprintf("%s (%d common evaluation date%s).",
                                 caption, n_dates, if (n_dates == 1) "" else "s"))
}

# A one-line convergence footnote for a disease: which competitors failed to
# converge, and on how many of the evaluation dates.
conv_note <- function(dis) {
  if (is.null(conv_df)) return("")
  bad <- conv_df[conv_df$disease == dis & conv_df$failed > 0, , drop = FALSE]
  if (nrow(bad) == 0) return("")
  items <- sprintf("%s failed on %d of %d dates", bad$model, bad$failed, bad$total)
  paste0("> _Convergence over the ", bad$total[1], " evaluation dates: ",
         paste(items, collapse = "; "),
         ". The comparison table uses each method's most reliable variant(s) on the dates they share._")
}
```

```{r no-scores-note, eval = !have_scores, echo = FALSE, results = "asis"}
cat("> _The pre-computed benchmark scores were not found (or are in an older",
    "format) in this build, so the score tables below are omitted. Regenerate",
    "them with `devel/benchmark_full.R` and copy `devel/results/*.rds` into",
    "`inst/extdata/`._\n")
```

For each disease there are four tables: (1) **every `diseasenowcasting` model**,
so you can see the spread; (2) **`diseasenowcasting` vs NobBS**; (3)
**`diseasenowcasting` vs epinowcast**; and (4) **`diseasenowcasting` vs
baselinenowcast**. In every table, **lower WIS = more accurate**, and you want
coverage close to its stated level (0.90 for `Cov90`). The footnote under each
table notes any competitor that failed to fit on some dates.


## Nobbs

### Dengue fever (weekly, Colombia)

```{r dengue-nobbs, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("dengue", c("nobbs","epinowcast","baselinenowcast"), "Dengue: diseasenowcasting vs NobBS")
```


```{r dengue-note, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores, results = "asis"}
cat(conv_note("dengue"))
```

**Key findings (dengue):** the `diseasenowcasting` HSGP variants take the top
spots, beating both NobBS and the best Epinowcast variant on WIS. Best model:
**HSGP / LogNormal** (WIS $\approx$ 7, cov90 $\approx$ 0.94).

### Mpox (daily, USA)


```{r mpox-nobbs, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("mpox", "nobbs", "Mpox: diseasenowcasting vs NobBS")
```

```{r mpox-enw, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("mpox", "epinowcast",
           "Mpox: diseasenowcasting vs epinowcast")
```

```{r mpox-base, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("mpox", "baselinenowcast",
           "Mpox: diseasenowcasting vs baselinenowcast")
```

```{r mpox-note, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores, results = "asis"}
cat(conv_note("mpox"))
```

**Key findings (mpox):** the `diseasenowcasting` Generalized-Gamma variants
achieve the lowest WIS, roughly **2Ă— better than NobBS** and well ahead of
Epinowcast.

### COVID-19 (daily, Colombia)

```{r covid-nobbs, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("covid", "nobbs", "COVID-19: diseasenowcasting vs NobBS")
```

```{r covid-enw, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("covid", "epinowcast",
           "COVID-19: diseasenowcasting vs epinowcast")
```

```{r covid-base, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores}
view_table("covid", "baselinenowcast",
           "COVID-19: diseasenowcasting vs baselinenowcast")
```

```{r covid-note, echo = FALSE, eval = have_scores, results = "asis"}
cat(conv_note("covid"))
```

**Key findings (COVID-19):** `diseasenowcasting` again leads on WIS, though the
margin over NobBS depends on the delay family.

## Conclusion

Across three diseases with very different reporting patterns, scored against the
counts that eventually arrived, **`diseasenowcasting` matched or beat NobBS,
epinowcast and baselinenowcast** on accuracy (WIS) while keeping its uncertainty
intervals trustworthy. 

## How to reproduce

Everything lives in a single self-contained script:

```
devel/benchmark_full.R
```

Run it (after `R CMD INSTALL` of this package, so the parallel workers can load
it):

```sh
RUN_AUTO=FALSE Rscript devel/benchmark_full.R                   # skip auto_nowcast (faster)
```

> Requirements: `NobBS` and `epinowcast` (the latter needs `cmdstanr` + a working
> CmdStan). The COVID series uses the aggregated Colombia data at the path in the
> `COVID_RDS` environment variable.

> **Note.** Earlier this section inlined a *simplified* reimplementation that
> fit only three own models, a single Epinowcast variant, and ran the own models
> through `backtest()` **without** the `X` covariates â€” which understated the
> diseasenowcasting models and inflated NobBS. `devel/benchmark_full.R` is the
> faithful, covariate-aware pipeline that reproduces the tables above.

## References
